Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. - simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). - schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. - run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). file.content:
• Project Atlas

Assignment

Take-Home Exercise - Profile Builder

Role: Full-Stack Engineer
Estimated time: ~2 hours
Stack: Your choice; we use Next.js + TypeScript, and you'll have the smoothest review if you stay close to that, but pick what lets you do your best work in 2 hours.

Why we're asking you to do this

We want to see how you build, not just what you build. In 2 hours you can't ship a polished product, and we're not asking you to. We're asking you to make a small, real thing well; with the same care for naming, structure, commits, and trade-off explanations that you'd bring to a feature on your first week at Atlas.

You can (and should) use whatever AI coding tools you normally use. We're more interested in your judgment, taste, and clarity than your typing speed. The deliverable here is your process as much as the artifact.

LLM Provider and Credentials

We won't provide an API key; provider choice and integration are part of the exercise. Use any LLM you want. Make the provider/model configurable, keep secrets out of the repo, and document setup in your README so we can see which provider/model you used, what env vars are required, and what happens when credentials are missing or invalid.

You don't need a paid key. Most major providers have free tiers or trial credits. OpenRouter has free models (https://openrouter.ai/collections/free-models) but they vary in quality and tool-call reliability. If you go with a flaky free model, that shows up in how robust your app is; document the trade-off.

The Task

Build a single-page web app called Profile Builder that helps a traveler create a durable "travel profile" through conversation.

The shape of it:

  • A chat interface where the user talks to the assistant about their travel preferences.

  • As the conversation progresses, the assistant extracts structured fields and builds a profile.

  • A read-only view in the UI that lists the current travel preferences. Side panel, section below the chat, separate page — your call on placement, but it should be part of the app, not a debug route.

  • The profile persists across reloads.


Must have

  • A working chat UI with streamed assistant responses (tokens appear as they arrive).

  • The assistant extracts structured profile data from the conversation. Pick your approach: tool/function calling, structured outputs, JSON mode, a second extraction call, whatever you can defend.

  • The profile persists (SQLite, a JSON file, Vercel KV, Postgres, or any other) and survives a page reload.

  • A read-only view in the UI that lists the current travel preferences. Placement is your call (panel, section, separate page), but it has to be part of the app.

  • A typed contract between client and server. We should be able to read your API and know what's flowing.

  • A destination tool your app calls. Whenever the conversation references a specific destination, the app must call getDestinationInfo and use the result to ask informed follow-up questions. Contract example below.

  • Conflict detection. When new conversation contradicts an already-extracted profile field (e.g., the user said "vegetarian" earlier, now mentions a steakhouse), the agent must surface the contradiction rather than silently overwriting. How you surface it — ask the user, flag it in the UI, propose a resolution — is your call.

  • A README.md that gets us running in under 5 minutes and walks us through your decisions.

The destination tool

You'll implement this in your codebase; it's a stand-in for what would be a real travel data API.

async function getDestinationInfo(name: string): Promise<DestinationInfo | null>

type DestinationInfo = {
  name: string;
  bestSeasons: Array<'spring' | 'summer' | 'fall' | 'winter'>;
  knownFor: string[]; // e.g., ["food", "architecture", "hiking"]
  averageDailyBudgetUSD: { budget: number; midRange: number; luxury: number };
  visaNotes?: string;
};

Hardcode data for at least 5 destinations of your choosing. Return null for unknown destinations — and make sure your agent does something sensible with that case rather than hallucinating. Feel free to extend the schema if it makes the conversation richer; just keep the function name and the existing fields.

Native LLM tool-calling is welcome but not required. An explicit orchestration step (detect the destination in app code, call the function, inject the result back into the prompt) is also fine if you can defend it.


Nice to have (only if you have time)

  • Structured logs for LLM calls and tool calls (model, latency, token counts, request IDs). JSON to stdout is fine.

  • Live updates to the profile view as the conversation progresses (vs. only after the turn completes).

  • Handling for the assistant changing its mind or correcting earlier extractions.

  • Anything else that shows judgment about the experience.

Explicitly out of scope

  • Authentication, accounts, or any notion of multiple users; assume one user.

  • Real travel data or third-party travel APIs; getDestinationInfo is the only source of truth.

  • Mobile or responsive polish. Desktop only; "doesn't break at smaller widths" is plenty.

  • Bookings, payments, sharing, search history, or any feature beyond the core loop.

  • Test coverage. A few targeted tests on parts that would hurt if they broke is welcome; full coverage is not.

  • A "polished" product. Two hours is short; cutting scope intelligently is part of the exercise.

Your call (we want to see how you decide)

  • What fields belong on a travel profile, and why those.

  • How proactive the assistant is — does it interview the user, or does it draft a profile and ask for corrections?

  • How and where the profile view sits in the layout.

  • How you handle ambiguity, contradictions, or the user changing their mind.

  • Where you stop. Two hours is the budget; cutting scope intelligently is part of the exercise.


The Process Deliverable

This is the part most candidates underweight, and it's the part we read most carefully. The goal is to give us a window into how you think with these tools.


1. Full session transcript(s)

Every prompt you sent and every response you got back, in order. Most assistants can export this:

  • Claude Code: /export or share the .jsonl from the session

  • Cursor: export chat

  • Codex CLI: commit the raw session file from ~/.codex/sessions/, or use jinghan23/codex-export for markdown

If you used multiple sessions or tools, include all of them, labeled. If you used spec-driven development, include the spec files too. If your tool doesn't support export (e.g. inline Copilot completions, tab-completes), say so and skip it. We'd rather have an honest gap than a reconstructed one.


2. Annotated highlights — 5 to 10 prompts in PROCESS.md

Pick the prompts where you made a real decision: scoping the schema, choosing an extraction approach, debugging a streaming bug, handling a conflict case, deciding what to cut. For each, two or three lines on:

  • What you were trying to do

  • What the tool gave you back (briefly)

  • What you kept, changed, or threw away — and why

This is the highest-signal part of the submission. Treat it like a code review comment.


3. A short reflection (same PROCESS.md)

  • Your overall approach. Did you plan first, or dive in? Did you let the agent drive, or stay in tight control? Why?

  • What you reviewed by hand vs. trusted. Where did you read every line? Where did you skim? Where (honestly) did you ship something you didn't fully verify?

  • What you'd do differently next time.

  • If this had to run at scale. What would you change about model choice, prompting, schema, or eval strategy if this were serving 10k requests an hour? Don't implement it, just sketch your thinking.

4. The repo itself

A public GitHub or GitLab repo with the working code, README, and PROCESS.md. Runnable in under 5 minutes from a clean clone.


What we're evaluating

We care about your judgment in the loop, not your typing speed. Roughly in the order it shapes our impression:

  • The annotated prompts. Clarity of thought, willingness to push back on the model, good taste about when to write something yourself rather than prompting for it.

  • Commit history. Small, focused commits with messages that explain the why. One giant "initial commit" tells us very little. We're are dogmatic about being able to read your work as a story.

  • README. Setup steps that work. A short section on the decisions you made and the trade-offs you accepted. A short section on what you'd do with another day.

  • Code structure. Where things live and why. Separation between transport, business logic, and UI. Naming that doesn't make us hover over things.

  • Types and API contract. Are client and server speaking the same language? Are runtime boundaries (LLM output, user input, persisted data) validated? We're looking for taste, not maximum strictness.

  • Streaming and AI integration. Does the streaming actually work end-to-end? Is the extraction robust to the LLM occasionally being weird? How do you handle errors and partial state? How does the tool call thread back into the conversation — does the agent actually use what getDestinationInfo returned, or does it ignore it?

  • Conflict handling. Conflicts are a place where lazy implementations show up fast. We'll look for: do you detect the conflict at all, do you handle it in a way that respects the user, and is the implementation localized or scattered across the codebase?

  • What you cut and why. Two hours is short on purpose. We want to see deliberate scope decisions, not panicked omissions.

  • What you didn't build. A short list of known gaps and how you'd address them is a strong positive signal.

  • Provider choice and configuration. We are not evaluating which paid model you have access to. We are evaluating whether you made a reasonable provider choice, kept credentials out of the repo, documented setup cleanly, and handled model/API failure modes thoughtfully.

Be honest about what you didn't fully understand or verify. If you copy-pasted a chunk of code from a prompt without reading it carefully, say so. Don't ship code you can't explain.


Questions

If anything is unclear, make an assumption and record it or send us a note before you start rather than guessing.


Submission

Reply with:

  • Repo link (public GitHub or GitLab)

  • Transcript file(s)

  • PROCESS.md (in the repo is fine)

  • Approximate time spent. If you went over 2 hours, tell us where the time went; we'd rather know

Good luck! We're looking forward to seeing what you build.

content: Auto mode lets Claude handle permission prompts automatically — Claude checks each tool call for risky actions and prompt injection before executing. Actions Claude identifies as safe are executed, while actions Claude identifies as risky are blocked and Claude may try a different approach. Ideal for long-running tasks. Sessions are slightly more expensive. Claude can make mistakes that allow harmful commands to run, it's recommended to only use in isolated environments. Shift+Tab to change mode.

1	# Profile Builder Design
2	
3	Date: 2026-07-08
4	
5	## Context
6	
7	The assignment is to build a single-page web app called Profile Builder in about two hours. The app should help a traveler create a durable travel profile through conversation. The evaluators care about the working artifact, but they also explicitly evaluate judgment, process, commit history, README quality, typed contracts, runtime validation, streaming, AI integration, conflict handling, and clear tradeoff explanations.
8	
9	The design optimizes for a realistic two-hour implementation rather than a broad production architecture.
10	
11	## Goals
12	
13	- Provide a working chat UI with streamed assistant responses.
14	- Extract structured travel profile fields from the conversation.
15	- Persist the profile across reloads.
16	- Show a read-only profile view in the main UI.
17	- Keep a typed, validated contract between client, server, LLM outputs, and persistence.
18	- Call `getDestinationInfo(name)` whenever a destination is referenced, and use the result in the assistant response.
19	- Detect contradictions and ask for user consent before applying conflicting profile changes.
20	- Include a README and PROCESS.md that explain setup, choices, tradeoffs, and known gaps.
21	
22	## Non-Goals
23	
24	- Authentication, accounts, or multi-user support.
25	- Real travel APIs or booking flows.
26	- A production database or deployment architecture.
27	- Full test coverage.
28	- Mobile polish beyond avoiding severe layout breakage.
29	- A heavy autonomous-agent framework unless a concrete need appears during implementation.
30	
31	## Recommended Approach
32	
33	Use a lean hybrid orchestration design:
34	
35	- Framework: Next.js with TypeScript.
36	- LLM integration: Vercel AI SDK using an OpenAI-compatible provider configuration.
37	- Primary provider path: OpenRouter, configured by environment variables.
38	- Validation: zod at HTTP, LLM, and persistence boundaries.
39	- Persistence: server-side JSON file storage for the single-user assignment scope.
40	- AI flow: the model interprets messages and proposes structured updates; app code validates, applies safe updates, blocks conflicting updates, and persists state.
41	
42	This keeps the implementation agentic where it matters without letting the model directly mutate durable state.
43	
44	## Alternatives Considered
45	
46	### Heavy agent framework
47	
48	LangChain, LangGraph, or a similar harness would make the project look more explicitly agentic and could provide native concepts for tools and state. It also adds abstraction, setup overhead, and debugging risk inside the two-hour limit. This is not recommended for the initial build.
49	
50	### Direct provider SDK or raw fetch
51	
52	A direct OpenAI-compatible fetch implementation would minimize dependencies and maximize control. It would also require more custom streaming code and more plumbing around model configuration. This is not recommended because streaming correctness is a must-have and the AI SDK already solves it well.
53	
54	### Production database
55	
56	SQLite, Postgres, or a hosted KV store would be closer to production persistence. For this assignment, data modeling and setup overhead are not worth the time cost. The README should explicitly state that production would move state to SQLite or Postgres.
57	
58	## Provider Configuration
59	
60	The app should support OpenAI-compatible providers through environment variables:
61	
62	- `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, or a generic compatible API key if we choose a generic name during implementation.
63	- `LLM_MODEL`, for example an OpenRouter model identifier.
64	- `LLM_BASE_URL`, defaulting to `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1` if using OpenRouter.
65	
66	OpenRouter is the documented primary provider because it can route to many model families, including Anthropic models, while preserving an OpenAI-compatible API surface. Direct Anthropic API support is a future adapter, not part of the initial scope.
67	
68	If credentials are missing or invalid, the app should load normally and return a clear chat error explaining how to configure the provider. Secrets must stay out of the repo.
69	
70	## UI Design
71	
72	Use a simple two-column desktop layout:
73	
74	- Left column: chat transcript and message input.
75	- Right column: read-only travel profile, unresolved conflicts, and basic provider/model status.
76	- Header: app name and possibly a reset control.
77	
78	This layout makes the core loop obvious: the user chats on the left while the durable extracted profile evolves on the right.
79	
80	## Travel Profile Schema
81	
82	Start with a compact schema that is useful for travel planning without becoming a CRM model:
83	
84	- `homeLocation`
85	- `preferredDestinations`
86	- `tripTypes`: values such as relaxation, adventure, food, culture, nightlife, nature, family, and work.
87	- `budgetLevel`: budget, midRange, or luxury.
88	- `pace`: relaxed, balanced, or packed.
89	- `lodgingStyle`
90	- `foodPreferences`
91	- `accessibilityNeeds`
92	- `travelCompanions`
93	- `preferredSeasons`
94	- `constraints`: visa, mobility, schedule, dietary, or avoid-list constraints.
95	- `openQuestions`: clarifications the assistant still needs.
96	
97	The implementation can represent uncertain or missing fields as absent or empty, but should avoid inventing preferences.
98	
99	## Persistent State
100	
101	Use server-side JSON persistence for the assignment version. A single persisted state file is acceptable and likely simplest:
102	
103	- `profile`: the current travel profile.
104	- `messages`: recent chat messages.
105	- `conflicts`: unresolved and resolved profile conflicts.
106	
107	The app should validate loaded JSON with zod before using it. If the state file is missing, initialize an empty state. If the file is malformed, return a clear error rather than silently corrupting or overwriting data.
108	
109	This intentionally assumes one user, matching the assignment scope.
110	
111	## Destination Tool
112	
113	Implement the required function:
114	
115	```ts
116	async function getDestinationInfo(name: string): Promise<DestinationInfo | null>
117	```
118	
119	Use at least five hardcoded destinations. Good initial choices are Tokyo, Paris, Mexico City, Reykjavik, and Lisbon. Each destination includes the required fields:
120	
121	- `name`
122	- `bestSeasons`
123	- `knownFor`
124	- `averageDailyBudgetUSD`
125	- `visaNotes` when useful
126	
127	The function returns `null` for unknown destinations. The assistant must treat `null` as a constraint: it can ask preference-oriented follow-up questions, but should not hallucinate destination facts.
128	
129	Destination detection can be app-orchestrated rather than native model tool-calling. The initial implementation should combine deterministic detection of known destination names with destination mentions returned by the structured extraction call. The server should call `getDestinationInfo` for the union of detected names before streaming the assistant response, then inject the results into the assistant prompt.
130	
131	## Chat Turn Flow
132	
133	Use extraction-before-streaming so conflicts can be surfaced in the same assistant response.
134	
135	```txt
136	User submits message
137	  -> server validates request body
138	  -> server loads and validates persisted state
139	  -> server appends user message
140	  -> server detects known destination mentions deterministically
141	  -> server runs a structured extraction LLM call
142	  -> server validates extraction output with zod
143	  -> server calls getDestinationInfo for each destination mention
144	  -> server applies safe profile updates
145	  -> server stores conflicts without applying conflicting fields
146	  -> server streams assistant response using profile, destination info, and conflicts
147	  -> server persists assistant message and updated state
148	  -> client refreshes profile state after streaming completes
149	```
150	
151	The client should not infer profile state from assistant text. It should render server state, either from chat response metadata if that remains simple or from a follow-up `GET /api/state` call after each streamed response.
152	
153	## Structured Extraction
154	
155	After each user message and before the assistant response, call the model for JSON-only extraction. The exact implementation may use AI SDK structured output helpers or explicit JSON prompting, but the returned shape must be validated before use.
156	
157	The extraction output should include:
158	
159	- `profilePatch`: proposed profile changes, which the app filters before applying.
160	- `conflicts`: proposed conflict candidates.
161	- `destinationMentions`: destinations the model noticed, used together with deterministic app detection before calling `getDestinationInfo`.
162	- `resolvedConflictIds`: conflicts the user explicitly resolved.
163	- `notesForAssistant`: optional short context that helps the streamed response ask better follow-up questions.
164	
165	Invalid extraction output should not mutate the profile. The app can still stream a conversational response if possible, while logging the extraction validation failure.
166	
167	## Profile Merge Rules
168	
169	The app owns durable state transitions.
170	
171	- Array fields accumulate unique values unless a conflict is present.
172	- Scalar fields can be set when empty.
173	- Scalar fields are not silently overwritten with materially different values.
174	- Conflicting updates are stored as unresolved conflicts and excluded from the applied profile patch.
175	- Explicit user resolution can apply the proposed value, keep the existing value, or keep both as contextual notes depending on the field.
176	
177	The model can identify semantic contradictions, but app code enforces the rule that conflicting durable updates require user consent.
178	
179	## Conflict Handling
180	
181	Conflict handling should be localized to a small profile update module rather than scattered through UI and API code.
182	
183	Example:
184	
185	- Existing profile: `foodPreferences = ["vegetarian"]`.
186	- New user message: "I want to visit great steakhouses."
187	- Extraction proposes a conflict for `foodPreferences` with a reason.
188	- App stores the conflict and does not overwrite `foodPreferences`.
189	- Assistant asks: "Earlier you mentioned vegetarian preferences, but now you are interested in steakhouses. Should I update that preference, keep vegetarian as a constraint, or note that steakhouses are for someone else?"
190	
191	The profile panel should show unresolved conflicts until the user resolves them.
192	
193	## API Shape
194	
195	Expected routes:
196	
197	- `POST /api/chat`: validates a user message, processes the turn, and streams the assistant response.
198	- `GET /api/state`: returns the validated current profile, messages as needed by the client, conflicts, and provider status.
199	- Optional `POST /api/reset`: clears local JSON state for demo convenience if time allows.
200	
201	Shared TypeScript types should describe request bodies, profile state, conflicts, destination info, and extraction output. zod schemas should validate all untrusted runtime inputs.
202	
203	## Error Handling
204	
205	- Missing API key: show a clear setup error and leave state unchanged.
206	- Invalid model or provider error: show a non-fatal chat error and leave state unchanged.
207	- Invalid extraction JSON: log the validation failure, skip profile mutation, and continue the conversation if possible.
208	- Unknown destination: call the tool, receive `null`, and make the assistant acknowledge limited destination data rather than invent facts.
209	- Persistence read/write failure: show a visible app error and avoid claiming the profile was saved.
210	- Conflict detected: store it, do not apply the conflicting field, and ask the user for consent.
211	
212	Structured logs to stdout are a nice-to-have if time allows, especially for LLM calls and destination tool calls.
213	
214	## Testing Strategy
215	
216	Keep tests targeted and high-signal:
217	
218	- `getDestinationInfo` returns data for known destinations and `null` for unknown destinations.
219	- zod schemas reject malformed extraction output.
220	- profile merge logic applies safe patches and blocks conflicting fields.
221	- optional API route tests if the setup cost is low.
222	
223	The goal is not full coverage. The goal is confidence in the logic that would most hurt the demo if it broke.
224	
225	## README Requirements
226	
227	The README should get reviewers running in under five minutes and include:
228	
229	- install and run commands.
230	- required environment variables.
231	- provider/model used during development.
232	- what happens when credentials are missing or invalid.
233	- core architecture decisions and tradeoffs.
234	- known gaps and what would change with another day.
235	- what would change at scale, especially provider choice, schema strategy, persistence, and evaluation.
236	
237	## PROCESS.md Requirements
238	
239	PROCESS.md should include:
240	
241	- overall approach and why planning came first.
242	- 5 to 10 annotated prompt highlights from the AI session.
243	- what was reviewed by hand versus trusted.
244	- honest gaps and unverified areas.
245	- what would be done differently next time.
246	- scale-up notes for serving many requests.
247	
248	The session transcript should be exported separately according to the tool's capabilities and referenced in the final submission.
249	
250	## Scope Cuts
251	
252	Cut from the initial two-hour implementation:
253	
254	- production database.
255	- authentication or multi-user state.
256	- native provider-specific tool-calling.
257	- direct Anthropic provider adapter.
258	- rich mobile design.
259	- broad UI test coverage.
260	- live profile updates while assistant tokens are still streaming.
261	
262	These cuts are deliberate and should be documented as such.
263	
264	## Success Criteria
265	
266	The design is successful if the final app demonstrates this loop end-to-end:
267	
268	1. User chats with the assistant and sees streamed responses.
269	2. The assistant asks proactive but natural travel-preference questions.
270	3. The profile panel updates after user turns and survives reloads.
271	4. Mentioning a known destination calls `getDestinationInfo` and informs the assistant response.
272	5. Mentioning an unknown destination does not cause hallucinated destination facts.
273	6. A contradictory preference is surfaced for user consent rather than silently overwritten.
274	7. README and PROCESS.md clearly explain the choices, tradeoffs, and known gaps.
275

- App stores the conflict and does not overwrite `foodPreferences`.
- Assistant asks: "Earlier you mentioned vegetarian preferences, but now you are interested in steakhouses. Should I update that preference, keep vegetarian as a constraint, or note that steakhouses are for someone else?"

The profile panel should show unresolved conflicts until the user resolves them.

## API Shape

Expected routes:

- `POST /api/chat`: validates a user message, processes the turn, and streams the assistant response.
- `GET /api/state`: returns the validated current profile, messages as needed by the client, conflicts, and provider status.
- Optional `POST /api/reset`: clears local JSON state for demo convenience if time allows.

Shared TypeScript types should describe request bodies, profile state, conflicts, destination info, and extraction output. zod schemas should validate all untrusted runtime inputs.

## Error Handling

- Missing API key: show a clear setup error and leave state unchanged.
- Invalid model or provider error: show a non-fatal chat error and leave state unchanged.
- Invalid extraction JSON: log the validation failure, skip profile mutation, and continue the conversation if possible.
- Unknown destination: call the tool, receive `null`, and make the assistant acknowledge limited destination data rather than invent facts.
- Persistence read/write failure: show a visible app error and avoid claiming the profile was saved.
- Conflict detected: store it, do not apply the conflicting field, and ask the user for consent.

Structured logs to stdout are a nice-to-have if time allows, especially for LLM calls and destination tool calls.

## Testing Strategy

Keep tests targeted and high-signal:

- `getDestinationInfo` returns data for known destinations and `null` for unknown destinations.
- zod schemas reject malformed extraction output.
- profile merge logic applies safe patches and blocks conflicting fields.
- optional API route tests if the setup cost is low.

The goal is not full coverage. The goal is confidence in the logic that would most hurt the demo if it broke.

## README Requirements

The README should get reviewers running in under five minutes and include:

- install and run commands.
- required environment variables.
- provider/model used during development.
- what happens when credentials are missing or invalid.
- core architecture decisions and tradeoffs.
- known gaps and what would change with another day.
- what would change at scale, especially provider choice, schema strategy, persistence, and evaluation.

## PROCESS.md Requirements

PROCESS.md should include:

- overall approach and why planning came first.
- 5 to 10 annotated prompt highlights from the AI session.
- what was reviewed by hand versus trusted.
- honest gaps and unverified areas.
- what would be done differently next time.
- scale-up notes for serving many requests.

The session transcript should be exported separately according to the tool's capabilities and referenced in the final submission.

## Scope Cuts

Cut from the initial two-hour implementation:

- production database.
- authentication or multi-user state.
- native provider-specific tool-calling.
- direct Anthropic provider adapter.
- rich mobile design.
- broad UI test coverage.
- live profile updates while assistant tokens are still streaming.

These cuts are deliberate and should be documented as such.

## Success Criteria

The design is successful if the final app demonstrates this loop end-to-end:

1. User chats with the assistant and sees streamed responses.
2. The assistant asks proactive but natural travel-preference questions.
3. The profile panel updates after user turns and survives reloads.
4. Mentioning a known destination calls `getDestinationInfo` and informs the assistant response.
5. Mentioning an unknown destination does not cause hallucinated destination facts.
6. A contradictory preference is surfaced for user consent rather than silently overwritten.
7. README and PROCESS.md clearly explain the choices, tradeoffs, and known gaps.

content.text:
Task 7 (chat + profile UI) checks out structurally — I read `ChatPane.tsx`, `ProfilePanel.tsx`, `app/page.tsx`, and `globals.css` against the design doc and brief, then independently reran `typecheck`, `test`, and `lint` (all green, confirming the task-7 report). I also smoke-tested the live app against the already-running dev server on port 3000 rather than starting a second one.

That live test surfaced three real issues the earlier review pass hadn't caught (progress.md currently marks task 7 "review clean"):

1. **Orphaned failed turns, invisible on reload** — `app/api/chat/route.ts` persists the user message even when the LLM call throws, but nothing marks it failed. I reproduced this live: `data/state.json` had two consecutive unanswered user messages sitting from an earlier failed attempt. On reload, `ChatPane` renders them as normal sent messages with no reply and no error — indistinguishable from "still thinking."
2. **Zero server-side logging on chat failures** — the catch block in that same route calls neither `logEvent` nor `console.error`. I triggered a real 500 ("Provider returned error") against the live OpenRouter key and nothing appeared in the dev log — the one case you'd most want a trace for produces none.
3. **No recovery path in `ChatPane` after an error** — the optimistic empty assistant bubble and the failed user bubble stay stuck in `localMessages` forever (only cleared on the success path), with no retry/dismiss.

Minor/nice-to-have: no auto-scroll to the newest message as the transcript grows.

Everything else matches the brief and design doc (two-column layout, streaming via manual reader/decoder, profile panel fields, unresolved-conflict display, provider-status banner). The extraction flakiness I also saw live (e.g., "Tokyo" not landing in `preferredDestinations`) is inherent to the model choice and already honestly disclosed in PROCESS.md/README.md, so no new gap there.

# Executing Plans

## Overview

Load plan, review critically, execute all tasks, report when complete.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan."

**Note:** Tell your human partner that Superpowers works much better with access to subagents. The quality of its work will be significantly higher if run on a platform with subagent support (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Codex App, and Copilot CLI all qualify; see the per-platform tool refs in `../using-superpowers/references/`). If subagents are available, use superpowers:subagent-driven-development instead of this skill.

## The Process

### Step 1: Load and Review Plan
1. Read plan file
2. Review critically - identify any questions or concerns about the plan
3. If concerns: Raise them with your human partner before starting
4. If no concerns: Create todos for the plan items and proceed

### Step 2: Execute Tasks

For each task:
1. Mark as in_progress
2. Follow each step exactly (plan has bite-sized steps)
3. Run verifications as specified
4. Mark as completed

### Step 3: Complete Development

After all tasks complete and verified:
- Announce: "I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work."
- **REQUIRED SUB-SKILL:** Use superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch
- Follow that skill to verify tests, present options, execute choice

## When to Stop and Ask for Help

**STOP executing immediately when:**
- Hit a blocker (missing dependency, test fails, instruction unclear)
- Plan has critical gaps preventing starting
- You don't understand an instruction
- Verification fails repeatedly

**Ask for clarification rather than guessing.**

## When to Revisit Earlier Steps

**Return to Review (Step 1) when:**
- Partner updates the plan based on your feedback
- Fundamental approach needs rethinking

**Don't force through blockers** - stop and ask.

## Remember
- Review plan critically first
- Follow plan steps exactly
- Don't skip verifications
- Reference skills when plan says to
- Stop when blocked, don't guess
- Never start implementation on main/master branch without explicit user consent

## Integration

**Required workflow skills:**
- **superpowers:using-git-worktrees** - Ensures isolated workspace (creates one or verifies existing)
- **superpowers:writing-plans** - Creates the plan this skill executes
- **superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch** - Complete development after all tasks

1705	
1706	**Files:**
1707	- Create: `README.md`
1708	- Create: `PROCESS.md`
1709	- Modify: code files only if final verification exposes issues.

1713	- Produces: reviewer-facing setup and process artifacts required by the assignment.
1714	
1715	- [ ] **Step 1: Create README**
1716	
1717	Create `README.md` with this content, then update the model name if a different one was used during manual testing:
1718	
1719	```md
1720	# Profile Builder
1721	
1722	A single-page conversational travel profile builder for the Atlas take-home exercise.
1723	
1724	## Quick Start
1725	
1726	```bash
1727	npm install
1728	cp .env.example .env.local
1729	npm run dev
1730	```
1731	
1732	Open `http://localhost:3000`.
1733	
1734	## LLM Configuration
1735	
1736	The app is configured for OpenAI-compatible providers through OpenRouter.
1737	
1738	Required in `.env.local`:
1739	
1740	```bash
1741	OPENROUTER_API_KEY=your_key_here
1742	LLM_MODEL=openai/gpt-4o-mini
1743	LLM_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
1744	```
1745	
1746	OpenRouter can route to many model families, including Anthropic models, by changing `LLM_MODEL`. Direct Anthropic API support would be a small future provider adapter, not part of this two-hour implementation.
1747	
1748	If credentials are missing or invalid, the UI still loads and chat requests return a clear setup error. Profile state is not mutated when the LLM call fails.
1749	
1750	## Architecture
1751	
1752	- Next.js + TypeScript single-page app.
1753	- Vercel AI SDK for streaming chat and structured extraction.
1754	- zod validates HTTP requests, LLM extraction output, and persisted JSON state.
1755	- Server-side JSON persistence in `data/state.json` for the assignment's single-user scope.
1756	- Hybrid agentic orchestration: the model interprets messages and proposes profile updates; app code validates and owns durable mutation.
1757	
1758	## Destination Tool
1759	
1760	`getDestinationInfo(name)` is implemented with hardcoded data for Tokyo, Paris, Mexico City, Reykjavik, and Lisbon. Unknown destinations return `null`; the assistant is instructed not to invent facts when tool data is unavailable.
1761	
1762	## Conflict Handling
1763	
1764	The extraction step can propose conflicts. App code stores those conflicts and blocks conflicting profile field updates until the user clarifies. This avoids silent overwrites of durable preferences.
1765	
1766	## Tradeoffs
1767	
1768	- JSON files instead of Postgres or SQLite to fit the two-hour scope.
1769	- App-orchestrated destination calls instead of native provider tool-calling for portability across OpenRouter models.
1770	- Extraction before streaming so the assistant can ask conflict-resolution questions in the same turn.
1771	- The profile panel refreshes after each streamed turn; live profile updates during token streaming are left out.
1772	
1773	## Testing
1774	
1775	```bash
1776	npm test
1777	npm run typecheck
1778	npm run build
1779	```
1780	
1781	Targeted tests cover destination lookup, zod schemas, persistence, and profile merge/conflict behavior.
1782	
1783	## Known Gaps
1784	
1785	- Single-user local JSON persistence only.
1786	- No auth, bookings, payments, or real travel APIs.
1787	- Conflict resolution is functional but intentionally simple.
1788	- Direct Anthropic provider support is not implemented.
1789	- The demo favors desktop layout.
1790	
1791	## With Another Day
1792	
1793	- Move persistence to SQLite or Postgres.
1794	- Add richer conflict-resolution UI controls.
1795	- Add eval fixtures for extraction and conflict detection.
1796	- Add direct provider adapters for Anthropic and OpenAI.
1797	- Improve conflict-resolution UI controls.
1798	
1799	## At Scale
1800	
1801	For 10k requests/hour, I would move profile and chat state to Postgres, add request tracing and structured model logs, introduce extraction evals before model changes, separate streaming chat from background profile extraction when latency matters, and add provider fallbacks/rate-limit handling.
1802	```
1803	
1804	- [ ] **Step 2: Create PROCESS.md**
1805	
1806	Create `PROCESS.md` with this content and adjust the prompt highlights to match the final exported transcript:
1807	
1808	```md
1809	# Process
1810	
1811	## Overall Approach
1812	
1813	I planned first because the assignment explicitly evaluates judgment, tradeoffs, and process artifacts. The design prioritizes a working, explainable two-hour slice over production infrastructure.
1814	
1815	I kept the agentic boundary narrow: the LLM interprets the conversation and proposes profile changes, while application code validates and owns durable state.
1816	
1817	## Annotated Prompt Highlights
1818	
1819	1. **Scope and stack selection**
1820	   I asked the agent to read the assignment and help choose a two-hour implementation strategy. I kept the recommendation to use Next.js and TypeScript because it matches the company's stack and reduces review friction.
1821	
1822	2. **Persistence tradeoff**
1823	   We compared Postgres/SQLite with server-side JSON. I chose JSON because the assignment assumes one user and values scope control; I documented the production database path instead of building it.
1824	
1825	3. **Agent framework tradeoff**
1826	   I questioned whether avoiding an agent harness would make the solution less agentic. I kept a hybrid approach: model-led interpretation, app-owned validation and mutation.
1827	
1828	4. **Conflict handling design**
1829	   We explored whether the model should directly update the profile. I rejected direct mutation because silent overwrites would fail the assignment's conflict-handling requirement.
1830	
1831	5. **Extraction and streaming order**
1832	   We compared streaming first versus extraction first. I chose extraction before streaming so the assistant can ask for conflict consent in the same turn.
1833

### Task 8: README, PROCESS, And Final Verification

**Files:**
- Create: `README.md`
- Create: `PROCESS.md`
- Modify: code files only if final verification exposes issues.

**Interfaces:**
- Consumes: final app behavior and design spec.
- Produces: reviewer-facing setup and process artifacts required by the assignment.

- [ ] **Step 1: Create README**

Create `README.md` with this content, then update the model name if a different one was used during manual testing:

```md
# Profile Builder

A single-page conversational travel profile builder for the Atlas take-home exercise.

## Quick Start

```bash
npm install
cp .env.example .env.local
npm run dev
```

Open `http://localhost:3000`.

## LLM Configuration

The app is configured for OpenAI-compatible providers through OpenRouter.

Required in `.env.local`:

```bash
OPENROUTER_API_KEY=your_key_here
LLM_MODEL=openai/gpt-4o-mini
LLM_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
```

OpenRouter can route to many model families, including Anthropic models, by changing `LLM_MODEL`. Direct Anthropic API support would be a small future provider adapter, not part of this two-hour implementation.

If credentials are missing or invalid, the UI still loads and chat requests return a clear setup error. Profile state is not mutated when the LLM call fails.

## Architecture

- Next.js + TypeScript single-page app.
- Vercel AI SDK for streaming chat and structured extraction.
- zod validates HTTP requests, LLM extraction output, and persisted JSON state.
- Server-side JSON persistence in `data/state.json` for the assignment's single-user scope.
- Hybrid agentic orchestration: the model interprets messages and proposes profile updates; app code validates and owns durable mutation.

## Destination Tool

`getDestinationInfo(name)` is implemented with hardcoded data for Tokyo, Paris, Mexico City, Reykjavik, and Lisbon. Unknown destinations return `null`; the assistant is instructed not to invent facts when tool data is unavailable.

## Conflict Handling

The extraction step can propose conflicts. App code stores those conflicts and blocks conflicting profile field updates until the user clarifies. This avoids silent overwrites of durable preferences.

## Tradeoffs

- JSON files instead of Postgres or SQLite to fit the two-hour scope.
- App-orchestrated destination calls instead of native provider tool-calling for portability across OpenRouter models.
- Extraction before streaming so the assistant can ask conflict-resolution questions in the same turn.
- The profile panel refreshes after each streamed turn; live profile updates during token streaming are left out.

## Testing

```bash
npm test
npm run typecheck
npm run build
```

Targeted tests cover destination lookup, zod schemas, persistence, and profile merge/conflict behavior.

## Known Gaps

- Single-user local JSON persistence only.
- No auth, bookings, payments, or real travel APIs.
- Conflict resolution is functional but intentionally simple.
- Direct Anthropic provider support is not implemented.
- The demo favors desktop layout.

## With Another Day

- Move persistence to SQLite or Postgres.
- Add richer conflict-resolution UI controls.
- Add eval fixtures for extraction and conflict detection.
- Add direct provider adapters for Anthropic and OpenAI.
- Improve conflict-resolution UI controls.

## At Scale

For 10k requests/hour, I would move profile and chat state to Postgres, add request tracing and structured model logs, introduce extraction evals before model changes, separate streaming chat from background profile extraction when latency matters, and add provider fallbacks/rate-limit handling.
```

- [ ] **Step 2: Create PROCESS.md**

Create `PROCESS.md` with this content and adjust the prompt highlights to match the final exported transcript:

```md
# Process

## Overall Approach

I planned first because the assignment explicitly evaluates judgment, tradeoffs, and process artifacts. The design prioritizes a working, explainable two-hour slice over production infrastructure.

I kept the agentic boundary narrow: the LLM interprets the conversation and proposes profile changes, while application code validates and owns durable state.

## Annotated Prompt Highlights

1. **Scope and stack selection**
   I asked the agent to read the assignment and help choose a two-hour implementation strategy. I kept the recommendation to use Next.js and TypeScript because it matches the company's stack and reduces review friction.

2. **Persistence tradeoff**
   We compared Postgres/SQLite with server-side JSON. I chose JSON because the assignment assumes one user and values scope control; I documented the production database path instead of building it.

3. **Agent framework tradeoff**
   I questioned whether avoiding an agent harness would make the solution less agentic. I kept a hybrid approach: model-led interpretation, app-owned validation and mutation.

4. **Conflict handling design**
   We explored whether the model should directly update the profile. I rejected direct mutation because silent overwrites would fail the assignment's conflict-handling requirement.

5. **Extraction and streaming order**
   We compared streaming first versus extraction first. I chose extraction before streaming so the assistant can ask for conflict consent in the same turn.

6. **Destination tool strategy**
   We chose app-orchestrated destination calls rather than native tool-calling. This keeps provider/model configuration portable across OpenRouter models.

7. **Schema size**
   We kept a compact travel profile schema rather than adding loyalty programs, passport status, or detailed deal preferences. This avoided over-modeling before the core loop worked.

## What I Reviewed By Hand

- Assignment requirements and evaluation criteria.
- The design spec and implementation plan.
- Profile schema and conflict merge rules.
- LLM boundary prompts and zod validation points.

## What I Trusted More Heavily

- Framework scaffolding conventions.
- Basic UI styling.
- Standard package wiring, after running verification commands.

## Known Gaps And Honesty Notes

- This is not production persistence.
- Free or cheap OpenRouter models can be flaky with structured extraction.
- Conflict detection depends on the model identifying semantic contradictions; app code enforces consent after a conflict is proposed.
- Assistant message persistence depends on the custom stream wrapper completing successfully after the model stream finishes.

## What I Would Do Differently Next Time

- Start with a small extraction eval fixture set before prompt tuning.
- Add explicit UI controls for accepting or rejecting each conflict.
- Add richer assistant-message audit metadata, including model and latency.

## If This Served 10k Requests An Hour

- Use Postgres for profiles, messages, conflicts, and audit trails.
- Add structured tracing for every LLM call and tool call.
- Run extraction evals before changing prompts or models.
- Use provider fallback and rate-limit handling.
- Consider separating streamed chat from asynchronous profile extraction for lower perceived latency.
```

- Message about an unknown destination produces an assistant response that does not invent destination facts.

```bash
git add README.md PROCESS.md
git commit -m "docs: explain setup and process"
```

Run: `git status --short`

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /home/matt/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/6.1.1/skills/finishing-a-development-branch

# Finishing a Development Branch

## Overview

Guide completion of development work by presenting clear options and handling chosen workflow.

**Core principle:** Verify tests → Detect environment → Present options → Execute choice → Clean up.

**Announce at start:** "I'm using the finishing-a-development-branch skill to complete this work."

## The Process

### Step 1: Verify Tests

**Before presenting options, verify tests pass:**

```bash
# Run project's test suite
npm test / cargo test / pytest / go test ./...
```

**If tests fail:**
```
Tests failing (<N> failures). Must fix before completing:

[Show failures]

Cannot proceed with merge/PR until tests pass.
```

Stop. Don't proceed to Step 2.

**If tests pass:** Continue to Step 2.

### Step 2: Detect Environment

**Determine workspace state before presenting options:**

```bash
GIT_DIR=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
GIT_COMMON=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
```

This determines which menu to show and how cleanup works:

| State | Menu | Cleanup |
|-------|------|---------|
| `GIT_DIR == GIT_COMMON` (normal repo) | Standard 4 options | No worktree to clean up |
| `GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON`, named branch | Standard 4 options | Provenance-based (see Step 6) |
| `GIT_DIR != GIT_COMMON`, detached HEAD | Reduced 3 options (no merge) | No cleanup (externally managed) |

### Step 3: Determine Base Branch

```bash
# Try common base branches
git merge-base HEAD main 2>/dev/null || git merge-base HEAD master 2>/dev/null
```

Or ask: "This branch split from main - is that correct?"

### Step 4: Present Options

**Normal repo and named-branch worktree — present exactly these 4 options:**

```
Implementation complete. What would you like to do?

1. Merge back to <base-branch> locally
2. Push and create a Pull Request
3. Keep the branch as-is (I'll handle it later)
4. Discard this work

Which option?
```

**Detached HEAD — present exactly these 3 options:**

```
Implementation complete. You're on a detached HEAD (externally managed workspace).

1. Push as new branch and create a Pull Request
2. Keep as-is (I'll handle it later)
3. Discard this work

Which option?
```

**Don't add explanation** - keep options concise.

### Step 5: Execute Choice

#### Option 1: Merge Locally

```bash
# Get main repo root for CWD safety
MAIN_ROOT=$(git -C "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)/.." rev-parse --show-toplevel)
cd "$MAIN_ROOT"

# Merge first — verify success before removing anything
git checkout <base-branch>
git pull
git merge <feature-branch>

# Verify tests on merged result
<test command>

# Only after merge succeeds: cleanup worktree (Step 6), then delete branch
```

Then: Cleanup worktree (Step 6), then delete branch:

```bash
git branch -d <feature-branch>
```

#### Option 2: Push and Create PR

```bash
# Push branch
git push -u origin <feature-branch>
```

**Do NOT clean up worktree** — user needs it alive to iterate on PR feedback.

#### Option 3: Keep As-Is

Report: "Keeping branch <name>. Worktree preserved at <path>."

**Don't cleanup worktree.**

#### Option 4: Discard

**Confirm first:**
```
This will permanently delete:
- Branch <name>
- All commits: <commit-list>
- Worktree at <path>

Type 'discard' to confirm.
```

Wait for exact confirmation.

If confirmed:
```bash
MAIN_ROOT=$(git -C "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)/.." rev-parse --show-toplevel)
cd "$MAIN_ROOT"
```

Then: Cleanup worktree (Step 6), then force-delete branch:
```bash
git branch -D <feature-branch>
```

### Step 6: Cleanup Workspace

**Only runs for Options 1 and 4.** Options 2 and 3 always preserve the worktree.

```bash
GIT_DIR=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
GIT_COMMON=$(cd "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)" 2>/dev/null && pwd -P)
WORKTREE_PATH=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
```

**If `GIT_DIR == GIT_COMMON`:** Normal repo, no worktree to clean up. Done.

**If worktree path is under `.worktrees/` or `worktrees/`:** Superpowers created this worktree — we own cleanup.

```bash
MAIN_ROOT=$(git -C "$(git rev-parse --git-common-dir)/.." rev-parse --show-toplevel)
cd "$MAIN_ROOT"
git worktree remove "$WORKTREE_PATH"
git worktree prune  # Self-healing: clean up any stale registrations
```

**Otherwise:** The host environment (harness) owns this workspace. Do NOT remove it. If your platform provides a workspace-exit tool, use it. Otherwise, leave the workspace in place.

## Quick Reference

| Option | Merge | Push | Keep Worktree | Cleanup Branch |
|--------|-------|------|---------------|----------------|
| 1. Merge locally | yes | - | - | yes |
| 2. Create PR | - | yes | yes | - |
| 3. Keep as-is | - | - | yes | - |
| 4. Discard | - | - | - | yes (force) |

## Common Mistakes

**Skipping test verification**
- **Problem:** Merge broken code, create failing PR
- **Fix:** Always verify tests before offering options

**Open-ended questions**
- **Problem:** "What should I do next?" is ambiguous
- **Fix:** Present exactly 4 structured options (or 3 for detached HEAD)

**Cleaning up worktree for Option 2**
- **Problem:** Remove worktree user needs for PR iteration
- **Fix:** Only cleanup for Options 1 and 4

**Deleting branch before removing worktree**
- **Problem:** `git branch -d` fails because worktree still references the branch
- **Fix:** Merge first, remove worktree, then delete branch

**Running git worktree remove from inside the worktree**
- **Problem:** Command fails silently when CWD is inside the worktree being removed
- **Fix:** Always `cd` to main repo root before `git worktree remove`

**Cleaning up harness-owned worktrees**
- **Problem:** Removing a worktree the harness created causes phantom state
- **Fix:** Only clean up worktrees under `.worktrees/` or `worktrees/`

**No confirmation for discard**
- **Problem:** Accidentally delete work
- **Fix:** Require typed "discard" confirmation

## Red Flags

**Never:**
- Proceed with failing tests
- Merge without verifying tests on result
- Delete work without confirmation
- Force-push without explicit request
- Remove a worktree before confirming merge success
- Clean up worktrees you didn't create (provenance check)
- Run `git worktree remove` from inside the worktree

**Always:**
- Verify tests before offering options
- Detect environment before presenting menu
- Present exactly 4 options (or 3 for detached HEAD)
- Get typed confirmation for Option 4
- Clean up worktree for Options 1 & 4 only
- `cd` to main repo root before worktree removal
- Run `git worktree prune` after removal

# Profile Builder

A single-page conversational travel profile builder.

## Quick Start

```bash
npm install
cp .env.example .env.local
npm run dev
```

Open `http://localhost:3000`.

## LLM Configuration

The app is configured for OpenAI-compatible providers through OpenRouter.

Required in `.env.local`:

```bash
OPENROUTER_API_KEY=your_key_here
LLM_MODEL=openai/gpt-4o-mini
LLM_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
```

OpenRouter can route to many model families, including Anthropic models, by changing `LLM_MODEL`. Direct Anthropic API support would be a small future provider adapter, not part of this two-hour implementation.

If credentials are missing or invalid, the UI still loads and chat requests return a clear setup error. Profile state is not mutated when the LLM call fails.

## Architecture

- Next.js + TypeScript single-page app.
- Vercel AI SDK for streaming chat and structured extraction.
- zod validates HTTP requests, LLM extraction output, and persisted JSON state.
- Server-side JSON persistence in `data/state.json` for the assignment's single-user scope.
- Hybrid agentic orchestration: the model interprets messages and proposes profile updates; app code validates and owns durable mutation.

## Destination Tool

`getDestinationInfo(name)` is implemented with hardcoded data for Tokyo, Paris, Mexico City, Reykjavik, and Lisbon. Unknown destinations return `null`; the assistant is instructed not to invent facts when tool data is unavailable.

## Conflict Handling

The extraction step can propose conflicts. App code stores those conflicts and blocks conflicting profile field updates until the user clarifies. This avoids silent overwrites of durable preferences.

## Tradeoffs

- JSON files instead of Postgres or SQLite to fit the two-hour scope.
- App-orchestrated destination calls instead of native provider tool-calling for portability across OpenRouter models.
- Extraction before streaming so the assistant can ask conflict-resolution questions in the same turn.
- The profile panel refreshes after each streamed turn; live profile updates during token streaming are left out.

## Testing

```bash
npm test
npm run typecheck
npm run build
```

Targeted tests cover destination lookup, zod schemas, persistence, and profile merge/conflict behavior.

## Known Gaps

- Single-user local JSON persistence only.
- No auth, bookings, payments, or real travel APIs.
- Conflict resolution is functional but intentionally simple.
- Direct Anthropic provider support is not implemented.
- The demo favors desktop layout.

## With Another Day

- Move persistence to SQLite or Postgres.
- Add richer conflict-resolution UI controls.
- Add eval fixtures for extraction and conflict detection.
- Add direct provider adapters for Anthropic and OpenAI.
- Improve conflict-resolution UI controls.

## At Scale

For 10k requests/hour, I would move profile and chat state to Postgres, add request tracing and structured model logs, introduce extraction evals before model changes, separate streaming chat from background profile extraction when latency matters, and add provider fallbacks/rate-limit handling.

# Process

## Overall Approach

I planned first because the assignment explicitly evaluates judgment, tradeoffs, and process artifacts. The design prioritizes a working, explainable two-hour slice over production infrastructure.

I kept the agentic boundary narrow: the LLM interprets the conversation and proposes profile changes, while application code validates and owns durable state.

## Annotated Prompt Highlights

1. **Scope and stack selection**
   I asked the agent to read the assignment and help choose a two-hour implementation strategy. I kept the recommendation to use Next.js and TypeScript because it matches the company's stack and reduces review friction.

2. **Persistence tradeoff**
   We compared Postgres/SQLite with server-side JSON. I chose JSON because the assignment assumes one user and values scope control; I documented the production database path instead of building it.

3. **Agent framework tradeoff**
   I questioned whether avoiding an agent harness would make the solution less agentic. I kept a hybrid approach: model-led interpretation, app-owned validation and mutation.

4. **Conflict handling design**
   We explored whether the model should directly update the profile. I rejected direct mutation because silent overwrites would fail the assignment's conflict-handling requirement.

5. **Extraction and streaming order**
   We compared streaming first versus extraction first. I chose extraction before streaming so the assistant can ask for conflict consent in the same turn.

6. **Destination tool strategy**
   We chose app-orchestrated destination calls rather than native tool-calling. This keeps provider/model configuration portable across OpenRouter models.

7. **Schema size**
   We kept a compact travel profile schema rather than adding loyalty programs, passport status, or detailed deal preferences. This avoided over-modeling before the core loop worked.

## What I Reviewed By Hand

- Assignment requirements and evaluation criteria.
- The design spec and implementation plan.
- Profile schema and conflict merge rules.
- LLM boundary prompts and zod validation points.

## What I Trusted More Heavily

- Framework scaffolding conventions.
- Basic UI styling.
- Standard package wiring, after running verification commands.

## Known Gaps And Honesty Notes

- This is not production persistence.
- Free or cheap OpenRouter models can be flaky with structured extraction.
- Conflict detection depends on the model identifying semantic contradictions; app code enforces consent after a conflict is proposed.
- Assistant message persistence depends on the custom stream wrapper completing successfully after the model stream finishes.

## What I Would Do Differently Next Time

- Start with a small extraction eval fixture set before prompt tuning.
- Add explicit UI controls for accepting or rejecting each conflict.
- Add richer assistant-message audit metadata, including model and latency.

## If This Served 10k Requests An Hour

- Use Postgres for profiles, messages, conflicts, and audit trails.
- Add structured tracing for every LLM call and tool call.
- Run extraction evals before changing prompts or models.
- Use provider fallback and rate-limit handling.
- Consider separating streamed chat from asynchronous profile extraction for lower perceived latency.

file.content:
• Project Atlas

Assignment

Take-Home Exercise - Profile Builder

Role: Full-Stack Engineer
Estimated time: ~2 hours
Stack: Your choice; we use Next.js + TypeScript, and you'll have the smoothest review if you stay close to that, but pick what lets you do your best work in 2 hours.

Why we're asking you to do this

We want to see how you build, not just what you build. In 2 hours you can't ship a polished product, and we're not asking you to. We're asking you to make a small, real thing well; with the same care for naming, structure, commits, and trade-off explanations that you'd bring to a feature on your first week at Atlas.

You can (and should) use whatever AI coding tools you normally use. We're more interested in your judgment, taste, and clarity than your typing speed. The deliverable here is your process as much as the artifact.

LLM Provider and Credentials

We won't provide an API key; provider choice and integration are part of the exercise. Use any LLM you want. Make the provider/model configurable, keep secrets out of the repo, and document setup in your README so we can see which provider/model you used, what env vars are required, and what happens when credentials are missing or invalid.

You don't need a paid key. Most major providers have free tiers or trial credits. OpenRouter has free models (https://openrouter.ai/collections/free-models) but they vary in quality and tool-call reliability. If you go with a flaky free model, that shows up in how robust your app is; document the trade-off.

The Task

Build a single-page web app called Profile Builder that helps a traveler create a durable "travel profile" through conversation.

The shape of it:

  • A chat interface where the user talks to the assistant about their travel preferences.

  • As the conversation progresses, the assistant extracts structured fields and builds a profile.

  • A read-only view in the UI that lists the current travel preferences. Side panel, section below the chat, separate page — your call on placement, but it should be part of the app, not a debug route.

  • The profile persists across reloads.


Must have

  • A working chat UI with streamed assistant responses (tokens appear as they arrive).

  • The assistant extracts structured profile data from the conversation. Pick your approach: tool/function calling, structured outputs, JSON mode, a second extraction call, whatever you can defend.

  • The profile persists (SQLite, a JSON file, Vercel KV, Postgres, or any other) and survives a page reload.

  • A read-only view in the UI that lists the current travel preferences. Placement is your call (panel, section, separate page), but it has to be part of the app.

  • A typed contract between client and server. We should be able to read your API and know what's flowing.

  • A destination tool your app calls. Whenever the conversation references a specific destination, the app must call getDestinationInfo and use the result to ask informed follow-up questions. Contract example below.

  • Conflict detection. When new conversation contradicts an already-extracted profile field (e.g., the user said "vegetarian" earlier, now mentions a steakhouse), the agent must surface the contradiction rather than silently overwriting. How you surface it — ask the user, flag it in the UI, propose a resolution — is your call.

  • A README.md that gets us running in under 5 minutes and walks us through your decisions.

The destination tool

You'll implement this in your codebase; it's a stand-in for what would be a real travel data API.

async function getDestinationInfo(name: string): Promise<DestinationInfo | null>

type DestinationInfo = {
  name: string;
  bestSeasons: Array<'spring' | 'summer' | 'fall' | 'winter'>;
  knownFor: string[]; // e.g., ["food", "architecture", "hiking"]
  averageDailyBudgetUSD: { budget: number; midRange: number; luxury: number };
  visaNotes?: string;
};

Hardcode data for at least 5 destinations of your choosing. Return null for unknown destinations — and make sure your agent does something sensible with that case rather than hallucinating. Feel free to extend the schema if it makes the conversation richer; just keep the function name and the existing fields.

Native LLM tool-calling is welcome but not required. An explicit orchestration step (detect the destination in app code, call the function, inject the result back into the prompt) is also fine if you can defend it.


Nice to have (only if you have time)

  • Structured logs for LLM calls and tool calls (model, latency, token counts, request IDs). JSON to stdout is fine.

  • Live updates to the profile view as the conversation progresses (vs. only after the turn completes).

  • Handling for the assistant changing its mind or correcting earlier extractions.

  • Anything else that shows judgment about the experience.

Explicitly out of scope

  • Authentication, accounts, or any notion of multiple users; assume one user.

  • Real travel data or third-party travel APIs; getDestinationInfo is the only source of truth.

  • Mobile or responsive polish. Desktop only; "doesn't break at smaller widths" is plenty.

  • Bookings, payments, sharing, search history, or any feature beyond the core loop.

  • Test coverage. A few targeted tests on parts that would hurt if they broke is welcome; full coverage is not.

  • A "polished" product. Two hours is short; cutting scope intelligently is part of the exercise.

Your call (we want to see how you decide)

  • What fields belong on a travel profile, and why those.

  • How proactive the assistant is — does it interview the user, or does it draft a profile and ask for corrections?

  • How and where the profile view sits in the layout.

  • How you handle ambiguity, contradictions, or the user changing their mind.

  • Where you stop. Two hours is the budget; cutting scope intelligently is part of the exercise.


The Process Deliverable

This is the part most candidates underweight, and it's the part we read most carefully. The goal is to give us a window into how you think with these tools.


1. Full session transcript(s)

Every prompt you sent and every response you got back, in order. Most assistants can export this:

  • Claude Code: /export or share the .jsonl from the session

  • Cursor: export chat

  • Codex CLI: commit the raw session file from ~/.codex/sessions/, or use jinghan23/codex-export for markdown

If you used multiple sessions or tools, include all of them, labeled. If you used spec-driven development, include the spec files too. If your tool doesn't support export (e.g. inline Copilot completions, tab-completes), say so and skip it. We'd rather have an honest gap than a reconstructed one.


2. Annotated highlights — 5 to 10 prompts in PROCESS.md

Pick the prompts where you made a real decision: scoping the schema, choosing an extraction approach, debugging a streaming bug, handling a conflict case, deciding what to cut. For each, two or three lines on:

  • What you were trying to do

  • What the tool gave you back (briefly)

  • What you kept, changed, or threw away — and why

This is the highest-signal part of the submission. Treat it like a code review comment.


3. A short reflection (same PROCESS.md)

  • Your overall approach. Did you plan first, or dive in? Did you let the agent drive, or stay in tight control? Why?

  • What you reviewed by hand vs. trusted. Where did you read every line? Where did you skim? Where (honestly) did you ship something you didn't fully verify?

  • What you'd do differently next time.

  • If this had to run at scale. What would you change about model choice, prompting, schema, or eval strategy if this were serving 10k requests an hour? Don't implement it, just sketch your thinking.

4. The repo itself

A public GitHub or GitLab repo with the working code, README, and PROCESS.md. Runnable in under 5 minutes from a clean clone.


What we're evaluating

We care about your judgment in the loop, not your typing speed. Roughly in the order it shapes our impression:

  • The annotated prompts. Clarity of thought, willingness to push back on the model, good taste about when to write something yourself rather than prompting for it.

  • Commit history. Small, focused commits with messages that explain the why. One giant "initial commit" tells us very little. We're are dogmatic about being able to read your work as a story.

  • README. Setup steps that work. A short section on the decisions you made and the trade-offs you accepted. A short section on what you'd do with another day.

  • Code structure. Where things live and why. Separation between transport, business logic, and UI. Naming that doesn't make us hover over things.

  • Types and API contract. Are client and server speaking the same language? Are runtime boundaries (LLM output, user input, persisted data) validated? We're looking for taste, not maximum strictness.

  • Streaming and AI integration. Does the streaming actually work end-to-end? Is the extraction robust to the LLM occasionally being weird? How do you handle errors and partial state? How does the tool call thread back into the conversation — does the agent actually use what getDestinationInfo returned, or does it ignore it?

  • Conflict handling. Conflicts are a place where lazy implementations show up fast. We'll look for: do you detect the conflict at all, do you handle it in a way that respects the user, and is the implementation localized or scattered across the codebase?

  • What you cut and why. Two hours is short on purpose. We want to see deliberate scope decisions, not panicked omissions.

  • What you didn't build. A short list of known gaps and how you'd address them is a strong positive signal.
